pr.title: Share boards with roles (viewer / editor / owner)
pr.subtitle: Jane Doe · 5 files

overview_html:
This PR moves boards from a single-owner model to membership with roles. A board is now shared with members, each carrying a role (viewer / editor / owner), and every endpoint is gated on that role instead of owner_id.

owner_id == user.id → BoardMember.role ≥ editor

What to read for

The old app/access/ownership.py is deleted and replaced by permissions.py (added) and members.py (added). The interesting question is the negative space: does anything still assume the old owner-only shape? The seam map and blast radius below record that check — each out-of-PR site was opened and judged, not assumed.

Good: require_view returns 404, not 403, so a board the caller can't see is never revealed; and delete-orphan on the new relationship cleans up membership rows when a board is deleted.

Watch: one file outside the diff (app/graph/dependencies.py) still reads the old shape, and there are no tests anywhere for the new authorization paths.

sites.note: deleted — logic moved to permissions.can_view / can_edit
new members / invite endpoints gated by role
MISSED — comment & guard still say 'ownership-checked'; a shared editor now reaches this path
no test exercises the new roles / sharing paths

files.note: MISSED — still reads the old owner-only shape
checked — already migrated to require_view, unaffected

entries.name: ownership.py
entries.walk: The old owner-only helper — deleted. Start here to see what the PR replaces.
entries.context:
This is the old access layer, and the PR deletes it entirely. It answered one question — are you the single owner of this board? — via owner_id == user.id. Everything that imported it (require_owner, is_owner, owned_boards) is migrated in this PR to the membership-based checks in permissions.py.

Because it's gone, the key review question is whether anything outside the diff still imports it — see the blast radius on the overview.
comments: Verified: no file inside the PR still imports ownership. The one out-of-PR reference is flagged in the blast radius below.

entries.name: permissions.py
entries.walk: The replacement: role-ranked access checks. This is the new source of truth.
entries.context:
The new access layer. A board is shared through BoardMember rows, each with a role; roles are ranked (viewer < editor < owner) so a single comparison answers “can this user do X?”.

Note the deliberate choice in require_view: it raises 404, not 403, so the API never reveals the existence of a board the caller isn't a member of.
comments: require_view raising NotFound rather than Forbidden avoids leaking that a board exists. Good instinct.

entries.name: members.py
entries.walk: List / invite collaborators. Two things to check: what list_members returns, and whether invite is safe to repeat.
entries.context: Membership management: list_members returns everyone on a board, and invite adds a user by email. Read this alongside the endpoints in boards.py that expose them.
comments: list_members exposes every member's email to any viewer

list_members returns m.user.email for every member, and the /members endpoint gates it on require_view — so any viewer can enumerate the email of every collaborator on a shared board. If that's intended for sharing UIs, fine; if not, restrict to editors or return only user_id.

(Also an N+1: m.user.email lazy-loads one query per member.)
invite isn't idempotent — re-inviting creates a duplicate row

invite never checks for an existing BoardMember, so calling it twice inserts two rows for the same (board, user) and still returns 201. Consider a unique constraint on (board_id, user_id) and an upsert, or a 200 on re-invite.

entries.name: board.py
entries.walk: The model change that powers the whole PR: the members relationship + BoardMember.
entries.context: Adds the BoardMember table and a members relationship on Board. The cascade="all, delete-orphan" is what makes deleting a board clean up its membership rows — the reason delete_board is safe.
comments: delete-orphan cascade closes the obvious leak
Membership rows are removed with their board. Without this, deleting a board would strand board_members rows. Nice.

entries.name: boards.py
entries.walk: Every endpoint migrated from require_owner to the role checks. The reader side of the seam.
entries.context: The HTTP layer. Each endpoint swaps require_owner for a role check (require_view to read, require_edit to mutate) and two new endpoints expose membership. This is the reader side of the seam — the place a missed migration would bite — so it's read line by line.
comments: list_boards loads every board, then filters in Python

Board.query.all() pulls all boards in the system into memory and then keeps the ones can_view passes — and can_view runs a query per board. On a large instance this is both a memory and an N+1 problem. Prefer a join against board_members filtered by user_id.
Authorization migration is complete and correct
All four pre-existing paths moved to the right check (read → view, mutate → edit), and delete_board's cascade comment matches the model. Verified against every call site.
No tests cover the new authorization paths

There is no test anywhere in the repo exercising 404-to-hide, the 403 on require_edit, or owner-vs-editor boundaries. Authorization is exactly the code you don't want to refactor untested — this is the highest-value gap in the PR.

entries.name: dependencies.py
entries.walk: NOT in the PR — but it still reads the old owner-only shape. A missed change, promoted to a first-class finding.
entries.note: This file is not part of the PR diff. It surfaced from the negative-space check (seam map + blast radius) and is included here because it should have changed and didn't.
entries.context:
This file was not touched by the PR, but it imports the now-deleted is_owner and its comment still claims the path is “ownership-checked”. After the migration a shared editor also reaches resolve(), so the guard is both stale and (once ownership.py is gone) an ImportError waiting to happen.

This is the class of bug the skill exists to catch: the change that should have happened and didn't.
comments: Stale owner-only guard on a now-shared path

dependencies.py:179 still calls is_owner (deleted in this PR) and comments the path as “ownership-checked”. Migrate it to can_edit and update the comment; otherwise a shared editor is wrongly rejected and the import breaks once ownership.py is removed.
